> plants LIKE this sort of water? ... because the plant life near the source water is amazingly abundant. If plants love it, why
> don't humans?

As Jack has discussed Deuterium is not the bad guy ,but it serves a purpose and has to be in the right place and not in a place that causes illness.

Deuterium is used in plants via there fruit and seed for propagation ...... survival/reproduce

### Sue-UK — 2018-12-11T12:59:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eat-anything-maybe.22626/post-257801>

> [JanSz said:](/goto/post?id=257744)
>
> Person who
> UNDER\_DRINKS
> **must be able** to make matrix water,
> otherwise under-drinking makes no sense.
> \---
> So,
> all other breadcrumbs given by [@Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) apply.
>
> ,,,,,,,,,,,,,,,

I think "must be able " assumes that mitochondrial function and heteroplasmy are good enough to be forced into a suitable response, coupling/uncoupling matched to environment, leptin sensitivity, that water loss to the environment is less than or matches what the matrix can produce, the level of body fat to begin with or sources of fat from food (without the disadvantages of chronic overeating) and whether there's any additional pressure such as a female needing to produce milk. Camels in cold desert conditions getting 10 litres a day from small regular doses from snow are possibly getting just enough and in a way to not jeopardise core body temp whilst sparing their body fat, which could have huge survival value. A starving polar bear (usually in summer) will drink water. The need for water forces animals to eventually risk the crocodiles, when it's that or die of thirst. There's no "must be able to make matrix water" decisions for them. As a human I can choose to ignore the signals my brain is giving me in relation to thirst, but the question is _should_ I? So far I'm a long way from being convinced it's a good idea.

### JackieNewZealand — 2018-12-12T01:40:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eat-anything-maybe.22626/post-257907>

Felt initially euphoric after starting CT, grounding, getting more sun and blocking blue light and perhaps went overboard on enjoying several different junk foods. I find that CT (cold therapy) REALLY increases my appetite and am not liking that. Have always had a fast metabolism and CT puts me over the edge.

Never had weight issues, but had auto-immune skin stuff going on (started while living in Australia) and think I'll go back to very low carb, maybe even carnivore way of eating. Too much sun also seems to make me look OLDER faster ... not good for skin. Maybe less sun, less CT and more keto eating. This is an absolutely fascinating interview with Jordan Peterson and his diet / depression / auto-immune issues::

I suppose you are referring to my constant use of "the three legged stool"

How many times has Dr. K. used the same response do you think?

Could it be that it is simple and concise and doable and nails it every time?

The three legged stool has been Jack's gift to us. It is great for newbies \[and all us non geeks\] who can't plough thru all the science and they can take action.

If you are complaining that I am a non geek ........you got me there. I can't give people detailed scientific answers \[ that is Jack's job BTW\] but I can read their journals and give them support and hope - lots of us have implemented the three legged stool and it has paid huge dividends.

everyone's n=1 is very different ....... I wouldn't dare presume to have detailed answers for someone's medical issues or put words in Jack's mouth.

If you come here for support - fantastic. There are lots of people here who put in many hours trying to lend a hand.

>
> Today is day 2 of burying myself in the sand at Tahunanui beach, then swimming my butt off in the not so warm surf. One can almost feel the electrons dancing into the body. He disconnected to reconnect in Mexico with himself.

He reversed his relationship with the oasis man got drunk with.

A good relationship is not a refuge from our problems, but an oasis where you can grow better as a person. Our relationship as humans were built with nature and technology is where we went to further our knowledge by pushing the relationship boundary. This toxic confrontation is what happened when our species began to be less than honest with ourselves and we got caught and began paying a toll to nature.
Our species used to use technology as an oasis, a pit stop to use its ability to help us further our understanding by using the power of the electromagnetic force.
Our species got drunk on this power like an alcoholic would at happy hour. We've built our world around technology now so that nature is now an oasis........and like an oasis in the desert, we need to visit that spot more often than not if we want to remain well. In the desert, the only god is a well. What we've done in such a short period of time to our species is astounding when you think about it.

### sailplane — 2018-12-21T04:25:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eat-anything-maybe.22626/post-258616>

> [sailplane said:](/goto/post?id=258615)
>
> I don't understand this study, .. it does not even list what "dietary fiber" means. Are they saying the persons on the no dietary fiber diet were eating absolutely no fruits or vegetables?

Ok I found it, "Patients were then instructed to completely stop their intake of dietary fiber, **including vegetables, cereals, fruits,** wholemeal bread and brown rice for 2 wk. " ... wow. That's quite a lot to take out. Do you really think it's a good idea to exclude all those foods just because they have fiber ?
I find that a medium amount of fiber works good for me, but a high amount does not. if I eat a lot of meat often(daily) and very low fiber at the same time then I'd have to use quite a lot of TP.

We weren't born with toilet paper, so wouldn't the perfect food result in a stool that you wouldn't even need any TP for? .. If it's sticky when it comes out, then it was sticky inside as well.. sticky makes me think of clogging and something not good.

That's just my experience.. maybe others can chime in with theirs.

### Cheryl. — 2018-12-21T09:54:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eat-anything-maybe.22626/post-258619>

### Inger — 2018-12-21T16:39:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eat-anything-maybe.22626/post-258636>

Î think right is, to eat what can grow outside where you live (not artificially grown stuff)
I live at the 50th latitude, and do not eat any veggies in the winter, except a few slices of raw onion most days. In summer I just eat very few veggies, almost none. I love wild berries eaten directly from the bush, I never got any issues with them, even if I gorge on them!
I have no issues having to use much toilet paper...lol
Veggies never did anything good for my digestion. In fact long ago, when I used to eat lots of veggies I had troubles... my digestion was not happy at all

There is a “revolution on the surface of the earth” called technology, and it is causing a new evolution of free radical signals in your mitochondria causing diseases. The result of these collisions creates your "health reality". Your health manifests from these collisions and creations.
Let’s use an example from human metabolism for a thought experiment: it is established that all so-called "chemical reactions" are merely the movement/expression of electrons/energy. Matter, the nucleus of the atom, the "nutrient" is inert in chemistry and biochemistry. At least that is the current belief of the biologic paradigm. I showed in my Vermont 2018 talk this is also false. This can also be found on Patreon. It is believed by the great majority in science only the electrons of atoms are dynamic, capable of change, force, movement. This is also not true. Protons move with the help of red light from the sun and the hydrogen heat furnaces of our mitochondrial colony. That is the first law we will call on to verify our thought experiment. Secondly, we call on this: it has been established that electrons gather/collect and only interact with photons. This is the basis of the photoelectric effect and is further understood as a cell’s Jablonski diagram. Electrons are excited by sunlight and fall back to the ground state. This process is capable of increasing their energy and delivering information to the system by changing the spin state of electrons, protons, and photons trapped by the organelles of a cell.
It turns out, electrons emit/yield photons to the demand of the body when the electron moves to another atom or molecule when it falls to the ground state. That light captured by electrons drives many processes in our cells. Electrons are more or less reactionary to the power density in the UV spectrum and use this information buried in these photons. This is the only part of the visible spectrum that is capable of using non-linear optics. Having established these two irrefutable principles, one must conclude: when there is a deficiency of some sort of enzyme, chemical, or substrate in a cell there is a resultant physical reactionary change. That change might be in a hormonal or enzymatic or functional aspect of the tissue in question.
Taking the exogenous substrate will never repair the deficits of having excited electrons creating these substrates. The reason is simple. Feedback loops use the substrates they create as their interactive controller of the cycle. Loss of this control causes the cycle to uncouple and heat is thermalized to the local environment and quantum coherence is lost. As coherence is lost redox power drops exponentially. There are physical manifestations of this in the matrix of size and shape change of the mitochondria, along with spacing out of the cytochrome proteins.
This can occur in a muscle or an organ within the organism. The skeptic will need to admit, the specific deficiency in this system of control is due to a lack or loss of photonic power within this quantum system. If one adds and moves electrons and protons properly, light within the visible spectrum is capable of creating the matter a cell factory needs to operate. That is precisely what free radicals are.

They are foremen in the factory that tell other worker proteins how to act. No one needs any supplement when you understand how a cell operates using light water and magnetism. To create matter a cell needs the cell must have redox power to do it. Optimal redox power is -400mV. Most humans live between -200mV—400mV.

Did you know deuterium biology and redox are fundamentally linked in nature? The linkage is non-linear and complex. Most people do not understand it well enough to make clinical decisions on it. Redox potential is the net negative charge developed in the mitochondrial colony you have. It varies from tissue to tissue but in humans, the redox in the brain and the heart are most critical because this is where our species stores its mitochondrial density and capacity.

Do you hear and often forget? Do you see and don't always remember? Do you experience, and still have trouble understanding? If this defines you, your redox potential might be too low in your colony of mitochondria in your brain. Some in this thread might want to read that last line a few more times before speaking in this thread again.

This picture below is how redox is codified in the mitochondrial inner membrane.

That -200mV is the limit of the biophysics of the red light chromophores in cytochrome C oxidase (which is the 4th cytochrome) in the picture below. It has 4 red light chromophores that are linked to specific frequencies of red light in sun. Sunlight has ZERO flicker, by the way.
When you drop below -200mV you REDOX is suffering badly. When this happens sleep apnea becomes MORE LIKELY. Solving sleep apnea is a big deal these days because of 4th Amendment issues and technology abuse.

Does eating a carbohydrate-laden diet cause sleep issues? Or can the light WE CHOOSE TO LIVE within doing it by itself?

The answer might surprise you.

For example, blue light overexposure causes high insulin and low glucagon in blood plasma without any food input needed: Both of these nnEMF stimuli raise AMPk pathways without any food intake. Nora Volkow showed this in 2011 and Alan Frey showed this in the 1960s.

Here is where nnEMF and blue link to the destruction of beta-oxidation on the inner mitochondrial membrane to foster a Warburg metabolism because redox drops below -200mV as the picture shows you.

You do not need food to cause it. It appears that sleep apnea and diabetes results from having the sun replaced by man-made light. This has altered the natural human habitat built under the sun.....

LED lights flicker more than any other man-made light and they cause many effects that mimic autistic-like behaviors and migraine headache. They also cause major sleep issues.........

What does flicker do to mitochondria?

Flicker allows too much deuterium to penetrate the mitochondrial matrix via UCP-2 and this slow ATPase spin rate from 9000 protons per second to something lower and that can cause your cognition to slow because the metabolic rate in your mitochondria is slowed by the kinetic isotope effect of deuterium in the matrix to slow ATPase spin rate from 9000/second. As the ATPase slows so does the size of the magnetic field created in our mitochondria and this is linked to lowered melatonin release from the pineal gland in darkness. It is also associated with a loss of superoxide burst at cytochrome one where NAD+ is used. This is where carbohydrate electrons enter mitochondria. DID you know NAD+ and melatonin are both made from the aromatic amino acid called tryptophan?

Did you know tryptophan needs UV light to be programmed to allow these two chemicals to work inside your cells PROPERLY?

Is this why taking exogenous melatonin of NAD+ supplements often cause more problems than not, because the exogenous replacements are never programmed by sunlight since they were made in a lab under fake light?

Did you know the deuterium nucleon makes full spectrum UV light? Full spectrum = UVA, UVB, and UVC light.

Did you know that those three bands are critical to the restoration of autophagy and apoptosis in mitochondria?

**Is this how deuterium biology links to Redox power in cells, Jack?**

Deuterium concentrations also lower the amount of CO2 and DDW the matrix makes and cause the redox to fall predominatly because it destroys endogenous glutathione production. You can see this link in the picture too! The only thing worse FOR REDOX that I have found to GSH loss is the use of exogenous glutathione, exogenous redox molecules like ASEA and the use of LED lights that flicker or doing bad things to eyes and skin like Lasik, glasses, tattoos. Things to think about today.................before you continue arguing about food some more.

Does deuterium have a zip code like nnEMF has one?

Is this why nature has 3 photosynthetic reactions webs that vary on the zip code of the planet?

Is this why this thread is filled with confusion?

Think to understand before you replay with some smart ass remark. Yes, I am fed up.

### DavidL — 2018-12-23T03:10:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eat-anything-maybe.22626/post-258811>

Talking about food is the easy way out. It’s like being in a class of the popular kids who aren’t very smart. The kids listen and look up to them because of the way they look and the clothes they wear. I used to be the cool kid. I was smart and would score at the top of every class if I wanted to. But I thought being cool was more important so I put my brain on the shelf and did what “cool kids” did.

This is exactly the state of the health gurus these days. Everyone wants to be popular, they want the clicks, they want the attention. It’s easy to dumb it down.

I remember when I was a young adult and the kids who weren’t cool were using their brains. I watched my peers rack up accolades instead of style points.

No one wants to learn biophysics because it requires time by yourself, reading, referencing, connecting dots. It’s not easy and it’s not popular.

I have a lot of pain walking in the morning but my local doc said the screws and plate does not interfere with the joint, so the pain isn’t coming from the metal (going from xrays). # Thread: Still haven't done any labs, but feeling rockstarish...:-D

### Maa — 2018-12-07T20:43:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/still-havent-done-any-labs-but-feeling-rockstarish-d.22633/post-257525>

7/9/18
Dr. Kruse,

You are God sent. The truth that you have been given and now give to us is truly light and life.

I read your book Epi Paleo Rx around February and immediately dropped all grains. Lowered carbs to a little Jackfruit (Jack It’s the most nutritious of them all, I hear. And so you are!) and a mixture of honey, pollen, propolis and royal jelly.

I have A2-A2 Jerseys, and drink the milk raw, use the cream heavily; the butter too. I’ve just now started to eat some sea food, but have been eating all the meats, eggs, with animal fats…

After being basically sedentary all my life, around Jan 17’ I stopped sitting down. I got up on the corner of a table on my hands, arms stiff, and started traction and back exercise. Taking weight off and on. My chronic back pain vanished. I also got on a mountain bike with an elevation simulating Training Mask, and rode 7 miles at just before sunset everyday, religiously. A real work for the chest muscles. Also got a set of gymnastic rings. I live on them. Listening to you and other stuff, talking; almost everything.

Wim Hof came to the farm in late March. Basically my first ice bath, I got in the garbage can that he just spent 5 mins in, and actually did the same\[FONT=Roboto, sans-serif\].\[/FONT\] I was in almost everyday for a couple months (but have gone to 50-55 degrees after hearing your CT blog).

Was feeling pretty darn good until about mid May. I started listening to your podcast page. I went thru about half of the latest ones, and then became a member, May 31st. Just yesterday, July 8th, I finished all webinars and Q&A’s. I now feel better then I ever have in body. Like the sphinx. I can’t be thankful enough…

I know I gotta do all my labs...

I have enrolled a couple friends and my oldest son. He really needs you. They proton therapied a tumor next to pituitary and apparently wiped the gland out… Hoping to get my wife and mother enrolled too. They really need it. I know you can heal them all, or rather, guide them to health.

Thank you Dr. Kruse, with all that is within me

12/7/18
Still haven't taken any of the labs yet...

# Thread: EHR = blue light hazard for docs = rustout and burnout.  No Shock
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ehr-blue-light-hazard-for-docs-rustout-and-burnout-no-shock.22643/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2018-12-09T03:03:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ehr-blue-light-hazard-for-docs-rustout-and-burnout-no-shock.22643/post-257620>

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

What if physician-facing user-interfaces were developed with the primary goal of taking better care of themselves by avoiding the blue light hazard. Do you think patients might get a collateral benefit??? What if the nurse didn't have to dig through a blue-lit device for hours on end to find out who's on call or what the rest of the Care Team was doing? What if the physician could subscribe to notification when sepsis warning signs started to appear -- instead of waiting for the lab to call the nurse to call the doc....? It's almost 2019, surely we can do better. We need all healthcare facilities to put IRIS software on the computers. These are just SOME of the healthcare challenges we're solving at Kruse Longevity Center.

Doctor use of electronic health records is causing stress that is strongly associated with physician burnout, according to a new study.

That’s the conclusion of an analysis of results from a 2017 Rhode Island Department of Health survey in which 4,197 practicing physicians in the state were surveyed on their EHR use.

Of the 1,792 doctors who responded to the survey (a 43 percent response rate), 26 percent reported experiencing physician burnout.

Researchers at Brown University’s Warren Alpert Medical School and other organizations conducted the analysis of the survey results, which were [published](https://academic.oup.com/jamia/advance-article-abstract/doi/10.1093/jamia/ocy145/5230918?redirectedFrom=fulltext) on Wednesday in the _Journal of the American Medical Informatics Association_.

The study focused on three health IT-related stress measures: whether the EHR adds to the frustration of a physician’s day, a sufficiency of time for documentation and the number of time doctors spent on the EHR at home = MASSIVE BLUE LIGHT HAZARD.

“Among EHR users (91 percent), 70 percent reported HIT-related stress, with the highest prevalence in primary care-oriented specialties,” state the study’s authors. “After adjustment, physicians reporting poor/marginal time for documentation had 2.8 times the odds of burnout, compared to those reporting sufficient time. Physicians reporting moderately high/excessive time on EHRs at home had 1.9 times the odds of burnout, compared to those with minimal/no EHR use at home. Those who agreed that EHRs add to their daily frustration had 2.4 times the odds of burnout, compared to those who disagreed.”

For the non physicians out there Primary care spends THE MOST TIME TEETHERED To the computer for the bean counters..........This is why your main doc.....the QB is getting their ability to think demolished.......(low dopamine = low melatonin = melanopsin dysfunction = rustout and burnout) No wonder why your outcomes are falling. Your docs are being devastated by the paradigm.

Overall, what researchers found was that HIT-related stress was measurable, prevalent and specialty related.

WHAT THEY DID NOT REALIZE IS THAT THE CAUSE WAS LIGHT MEDIATED BY THE BLUE LIGHT TOXIC EFFECT ON MITOCHONDRIA.

According to the study, more than a third of primary care physicians reported all three measures of EHR-related stress, including general internists (39.5 percent), family medicine physicians (37 percent), dermatologists (36.4 percent) and pediatricians (33.6 percent).

The dermatologist link made me chuckle........they blame the sun for everything while they themselves are being destroyed by the real villain.......BLUE LIGHT. If you want to avoid skin cancers and skin problems go outside in the sun.......bury the tech light, LED/CFLs and screens. LOL

On the low end of the scale were radiologists (1.7 percent) who reported all three measures of HIT-related stress, followed by anesthesiologists (2.7 percent) and hospitalists (5.6 percent).

Despite the fact that family medicine physicians (35.7 percent) and **dermatologists** (34.6 percent) reported the highest levels of burnout, hospital medicine specialists (30.8 percent) placed third.

Does anyone want to guess the trend is? It is the amount of time they spend under blue light from screens and in their work settings.

The moral? Get done fast in the hospital/office and reconnect with the sun and Earth to make up for the OSHA risks of blue light.

[https://academic.oup.com/jamia/adva.../jamia/ocy145/5230918?redirectedFrom=fulltext](https://academic.oup.com/jamia/advance-article-abstract/doi/10.1093/jamia/ocy145/5230918?redirectedFrom=fulltext)

### Jack Kruse — 2018-12-09T16:44:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ehr-blue-light-hazard-for-docs-rustout-and-burnout-no-shock.22643/post-257652>

Red light and purple frequencies in sunlight slow time down below the cell level. Cold temperatures are capable of also slowing time too but far less effective than UV/IRA light. Blue light speeds up time and your life is lived like a blue straggler star in galaxies. Blue star exists in a "quick and bright" way. They are the rock star of the cosmos; they burn brightly initially and die quickly. Over supplemented comes from being undereducated about light. I try to be a rainbow in people’s clouds, but I can’t solve their climate of understanding………..only they can when they come to my level of understanding. This is why I will never dumb down my thinking for anyone. They need to elevate their game by coming to my level.

Cytochrome 1 makes superoxide free radicals and H202 is made by cytochrome 2. Incident EMF from the sun makes both.......melanin quenches one free radical and catalase quenches the other..........Melanopsin dysfunction destroys the circadian feedback control arm of the cytochromes and free radicals. My QT#25 blog on Patreon tells you just how damaging this small effect is.........

If you are a Black Swan you'll read the wisdom. If you are a skeptic you'll choose to remain in the dark.

When setting out on a journey of understanding you should never rely on advice from those who have not yet left home. Being considered crazy is a complement to a Black swan because it points out how your critics have remained victims of the cultural conditioning of their education. It is a profound compliment and a badge I enjoy.

The beliefs of modern man are the batteries that make them fuel for the matrix in which they live. It is in the pauses, the intervals, the spaces between the words, that the meaning of what you are saying sinks in. To listen to a good talk, whether from a pulpit or a podium, whether off-the-cuff or seared into memory, is to hear a kind of music, not just in the register, the lilt, the cadence and the rhythm, but in those moments when there are no words to be heard, when all you can hear is the enveloping silence. The same is true with cells…..sounds are like sunlight and the silence is found in the darkness of night. The real music of life is found at night when light SHOULD BE absent. This is why ALAN, called artificial light at night is so dangerous to humans. [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/30496813](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/30496813)

Mam, if you've had a 401k, IRA, or pension you have profited and just not been fully aware.

Does a lion not notice an advantage when seeing a limping gazelle? Surely the lion shouldn't profit from the gazelle's illness?

Unless we'd like all pious people to never profit from idiocy but retain the right to decry how "They" always profit.

This is not a binary (zoroastrian) game. Failing systems and ill systems must be removed otherwise their demise will be catastrophic. That's a true moral imperative.

[@RobInDC](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/21869/) I was a little too tough on you in the beginning. I'm glad to see your thinking moving along here. ### ElectricUniverse — 2018-12-14T18:31:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quantum-stock-picks.22646/post-258104>

> [RobInDC said:](/goto/post?id=257936)
>
> Jack mentioned that both Pharma and insurance would go up. Trying to figure out why insurance might go up.

My understanding is that insurance companies, far from being unhappy by increasing insurance claims (from weather, accidents, sickness) actually love it because they have an ongoing excuse to hike premiums and make out like bandits in profits.

So, in _short term_ insurance industry looks like a good bet. _Medium-long_ term they will likely crash and burn. And here is why:

Biggest elephant in room is so-called health care insurance (and Medicare- Medicaid). It is an extremely wasteful and unsustainable segment of economy, growing from 13.3% of GDP in 2000 to 18.2% in 2018 (and still counting). It is bloated with red tape and high administrative overhead costs. It has little to do with keeping patients healthy and everything to do with enriching health industry participants and administrators.

Karl Denninger is a respected independent market analyst who has been sounding a shrill alarm on the unsustainability of health care cartel growth in USA and has the run the numbers and concluded that within a relatively few years it will be such a bloated drain on USA economy that it will bring it completely down. He says the only sensible way to rein it in is to allow free market forces to operate and introduce price-reducing competition.

Another head wind for insurance companies (as well as pension funds, etc) is that because the Federal Reserve has stomped on interest rates for so long (near zero rates until just recently) their bond holdings are hemorrhaging money. That's because their bond ladders hold bonds that must be reinvested when they mature. Bonds purchased 10 or more years ago at a decent rate have to be reinvested at piddling interest rates today. Insurance industry is getting clobbered in this area.

Safer investments like US treasuries are a part of insurance co and pension fund portfolios to offset volatility in stock markets.

If I were an adventurous investor (which I am not) I would look for opportunities to short insurance industry in near future (but not now).

### ElectricUniverse — 2018-12-14T18:46:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quantum-stock-picks.22646/post-258105>

> [recoen said:](/goto/post?id=257939)
>
> Or do we not invest and remain in cash and instead pay off debts? I am trying to figure out if I should put the $5500 in my IRA at the beginning of 2019 (and not invest it until the correction) or use it to pay off more of my mortgage... or do I buy gold?

I am pulling all money out of my IRAs, period. We are entering uncharted terrain in global economy-- with unbelievable amounts of debt in system.

Central banks and their client governments have been able to keep things propped up until now since the so-called Great Recession of 2008-09. But it appears we are again predictably sliding into another financial crisis and central banks have expended all their ammo. More money printing probably won't work at all this time.

A danger with putting all your wealth in cash or cash equivalents is that attempted recovery efforts might precipitate at some point hyperinflation. That would diminish or wipe out the purchasing power of your cash holdings.

Stock markets are manipulated casinos so we should be very careful in that area. In foreseeable futures stocks are likely to crash to unimaginably low levels. However, only fools make tight timing predictions.

I was never interested in gold but the shiny metal is starting to have an appealing sheen. Not as an investment (after all, it produces no income flow) but as a hedge. There is no third party risk with gold. It may hold most of its value in an economic crisis-- but we can't know with certainty. If you listen to gold pundits (and there are plenty of those these days) they are recommending something like placing 10% to 25% of of your net worth in physical (not paper) gold. Don't hold it in a bank where it could be confiscated in a crisis by authorities.

One of the best things you can do to prepare and help prosper in these uncertain times is to get out of debt. Did I mention get rid of debt-- all forms of debt including mortgages. Even if economy collapses don't think you debtors will get a pass. Banks will swoop in like vultures.

### ElectricUniverse — 2018-12-14T20:45:07+0200

Silver is really undervalued right now. It might be considered the "poorer man's gold", a precious metal for the average Joe or Josephine.

Its pros include the fact that silver is used widely in industry, while gold is not. So there is economic activity backing its value. It might also be easier to trade or barter with, especially with widely recognized silver coins like American Eagle.

Cons include that silver does not have as great a reputation as a store of value like gold has had for thousands of years. Plus the fact that it can take up a lot of space and weigh a bunch if you are buying a lot (imagine storing $75K of ounce silver coins at home-- be sure to support those floor joists).

Numismatic collectable coins are another interesting option for investors, as is 'junk silver'-- currency coins minted pre-1964 that actually contained silver as opposed to our monopoly money play currency coins of today. These types of precious metal products will probably fare well in a barter economy.

Ladies, don't forget that gold and silver-containing jewelry are assets too.

The MIC (military-industrial complex) will lose its luster when it comes down to a choice between maintaining essential government services and welfare versus feeding the giant maws of MIC.

You may say that we will still need people to build and maintain robots-- LOL, they will use still more robots for that.

I would avoid high tech (it won't solve our all problems and actually tends to create more societal problems than it solves) and focus on needful and useful services and products that actually help people. Use your imagination because humanity always has needs, and needs will get greater when the fat, dumb, and sassy people of western world gets their standard of living cut down a notch or two.

### ElectricUniverse — 2018-12-16T18:03:57+0200

> [axel brandt said:](/goto/post?id=258217)
>
> We are Still on a bear market for commodities ( like gold silver, oil ) they move on cycles, bear markets lasts 15- 20 years on average, bull markets 10. Last one ended in 1998, so expect low silver prices until at least 2023. It's better to own solar energy stocks, clean , safe and endless energy of the future.

Thanks for the input.

Markets can be shown to indeed move in cycles with varying wavelengths. Everything under the sun is subject to natural cycles, frequencies, and patterns. Natural cycles at best act in closely correlating intervals, or less predictably in generalized rhymes.

As you know, the problem with cycles that interact closely with humans, like economic ones, is that humans are subject to much more unpredictability. So exact market timing is best left to fools and charlatans.

Right now, at height of human folly, markets are unhinged from real world fundamentals and are running on central bank funny money fumes. That has to, and will, end. Probably much sooner than later now since we are so advanced in this manipulated economic cycle.

I would like to virtue signal that I am fully supportive of '**green energy**' like solar and wind but I can not.

Solar and wind have costs to environment-- solar because of the large amounts of resources and energy used in manufacture, ditto for wind but add in other costs like visual and noise pollution plus hazards to birds.

Both forms of green energy are likely to be much less useful if the new _Grand Solar Minimum_ cooling is as severe as they are predicting (right on cue with its own natural, repeating cycle as shown in historical records and earth records like ice core studies).

Solar will largely disappoint because of a dearth of sunny days on planet Earth, and wind turbine farms should be decimated as jet streams go out of phase and continue to wreck havoc with biblical storms.

So **_if_** I were investing in energy I would place bets on fossil fuels (CO2 is not heating the planet-- we are cooling) like coal, natural gas, and oil, as well as geothermal.

Proponents of Grand Solar Minimum (Mini Ice Age) don't argue that overall solar radiation will diminish appreciably. TSI, or Total Solar Irradiation levels are projected to drop only a fraction

The reason solar energy technology will disappoint greatly in the GSM is because sun's magnetosphere will be weak and its magnetic currents go out of whack. This causes more galactic cosmic rays to reach into earth's lower atmosphere and into crust (caused by earth's protective magnetosphere weakening as a result of solar changes).

As a consequence, cosmic rays will act as cloud nucleation accelerators, increasing the albedo of effect of more cloud cover on planet (this is happening already). This would be a main reason solar panel output will be muted.

Also, jet streams go out of pattern because of our weakened magnetosphere and consequently this can and will cause biblical-scale collision of air masses with destructive storms, floods, and epic snow storms.

Increase in snow cover in GSM will also be an albedo creator and cool us off more. Not to mention more frequent and violent volcanic eruptions that coincide historically with GSM solar cycles (again--culprit is cosmic rays as trigger). These eruptions will add more dust particles into atmosphere and prevent more solar energy from reaching earth.

Be thankful the world still has enough fossil fuel stocks to keep us warm and powered up in coming mini ice age, which could last for as long as 50 years or more. I would rather be safe and warm in a fossil fuel heated dwelling than freezing to death virtuously in a 'green solar' powered home.

But we have much to do to conserve this fossil fuel treasure to ensure future generations are left with enough to carry on society and enable continued search for alternative fuels.

### axel brandt — 2018-12-21T02:15:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quantum-stock-picks.22646/post-258606>

We can't, and shouldn't, continue using oil and coal forever — it is, as Elon Musk has said, "[the dumbest experiment in history](https://www.techinsider.io/elon-musk-talks-fossil-fuels-with-wait-but-why-2015-8)" because we are changing the Earth's atmosphere without knowing the consequences.

[https://www.businessinsider.com/this-is-the-potential-of-solar-power-2015-9](https://www.businessinsider.com/this-is-the-potential-of-solar-power-2015-9)

There's one simple fact that may just change your thoughts on renewable power.

"In a single hour, the amount of power from the sun that strikes the Earth is more than the entire world consumes in an year"

To put that in [numbers, from the US Department of Energy](https://science.energy.gov/~/media/bes/pdf/reports/files/seu_rpt.pdf):

Each hour 430 quintillion Joules of energy from the sun hits the Earth. That's 430 with 18 zeroes after it!

In comparison, the total amount of energy that all humans use in a year is 410 quintillion Joules.

For context, the average American home used [39 billion Joules of electricity in 2013](https://www.eia.gov/tools/faqs/faq.cfm?id=97&t=3).

Clearly, we have a source of virtually unlimited ([the sun won't die out for another 5 billion years or so](http://www.space.com/10245-sun-die-earth.html)) clean energy in the form of solar power — we're just not capturing it.

There´s no "albedo effect" that can stop the huge power of the sun.

Ok, let´s talk in one or two years.
thank you for your thoughts, this site is for exchanging opinions, we cannot think in the same way, it would be boring !

Good philosophy Emma.

Markets always run in cycles, even when Wall Street, the gubmint, and central banks think they have the power to repeal business cycles. Trouble is, when historic money and market manipulation rules the day making cycle predictions get real dicey. Not for the faint of heart.

That's why I am cooling my heels and waiting before plunging into market again. We are on fringes of bear market now, but I do expect some bear market rallies into 2019. When a recession becomes apparent later in 2019 the bull market enthusiasts likely will capitulate around then. That would be time to 'look out below!-- this sucker is going down'.

Honestly, virtually all asset classes-- stocks, bonds, real estate, you name it, are in bubble territory now. How it all ends I don't know but I do believe, as Peter Schiff is now saying, most Americans' standard of living will go in the pooper.

Best investments for right now for most of us of in the 99% are probably gold, silver, lead, debt-freedom, community networks, and prepper supplies.

Wow, I am impressed, Emma. From your information sources it looks like you are after the truth and serious about gaining an understanding of our economic situation. I don't follow any particular media pundit. I like to eat a buffet of alternative non-mainstream economy news sites and try to digest it (sometimes that causes indigestion).

Treasury note investments (from Treasury Direct), short term 4-8 week duration, are what I am going after now. They are about the safest places to park your cash now, although interest yield is not stellar at a tad above 2%. CD yields have wiggled up a little, but to get still measly returns a bit above 2% you have to commit your money to longer periods locked in a bank.

Main thing right now, since we are on the cusp of a sea change in economic fortunes, is to preserve your capital while looking to get a little return to offset simmering inflation.

**Patience** is the key to be able to prepare, prevail, and prosper. And I do wish everyone prosperity and freedom from misery of financial instability.

# Thread: Eating the Leptin rx, but tall, thin, low appetite
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-the-leptin-rx-but-tall-thin-low-appetite.22648/>

### MattD1995 — 2018-12-09T23:33:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-the-leptin-rx-but-tall-thin-low-appetite.22648/post-257676>

I’ve perused the forums for info on this topic. I know Dr Kruse has said both fat and thin people can be Leptin resistant. Here’s my problem...

I already have a fairly low appetite. While eating clean carbs like small amounts of basmati rice or potatoes, my appetite stays decent. However, eating low carb DESTROYS my appetite. Every time. I’ve eaten keto before. Carnivore. And now I’ve been on the Leptin RX with tons of seafood for 2 weeks, and I’m running into the same problem I have every time I eat low carb.

I’m rapidly losing weight. To much weight. The more weight I lose, the more fatigued I get which is counterproductive since my goal is to fix my severe Adrenal fatigue. I’ve always been a very healthy weight without even trying. 6 foot 8, 220 pounds, and a basketball build from years of sports. I’m getting uncomfortably thin though on this diet.

I’m doing daily CT, eating oysters/salmon/sardines, Grounding, wearing blue blockers, and getting sun. My appetite is continuing to drop and it’s affecting me both mentally and physically as my weight declines.

I’ve been obsessively consuming Dr Kruse’s blog/Facebook/Patreon posts for hours a day attempting to learn as much as possible. I understand WHY I should be eating the Leptin RX, but it’s the one protocol he advocates that my body just doesn’t seem to be able to handle at the moment.

I’m open to any input/suggestions. I’m currently looking into EFT and some other mental treatments to try to resolve any potential mental/emotional stressors that might be affecting my appetite.

Thank you!

HAIR GROWTH AND LOSS IS A LIGHT EVENT: Did you know that regrowing hair is just like watering a plant?

Hair needs to absorb light energy to stay healthy and fight the thinning process. Here’s the formula:

Scalp + Light Energy = Hair Growth

We can get hair growth when we add cooling of the environment with a lot of red light from a good source to increase our matrix engine. Hair grows as a result of the mitochondria function in the follicle. It also means that the more surface area you irradiate and cool the more hair you’ll grow. This is why winter animals get winter coats in case you were wondering. It is a simple Carnot example of how we really work. Baldness is a loss of mitochondrial efficiency from indoor living, hats, and lack of sun and cold exposure.

Carnot showed us a few hundred years ago how a heat engine like mitochondria really works. His work showed that the best way to increase the efficiency of a heat engine was to increase the difference in the temperature from the heat source (matrix) inside the engine and the exterior surroundings. The more mitochondrial damage you have the harder it is to regrow hair and the more light and cold you need to grow your hair back!!!

The hotter an engine can run the more thermally efficient it is. Red light exposure and cooling both increase the amount of heat your colony of mitochondria release in your scalp to grow hair. This is why diabetics lose hair all over their bodies as their HbA1C rises. Most of them are blue light or nnEMF toxic. This idea has guided engineers for 200 years but it still seems to perplex doctors who treat people with mitochondrial damage from many sources. Watch 16:90-18:00 of this video to get it loud and clear. BIOPHYSICS ALWAYS WINS THE WAR OF ATTRITION with the ignorant in medicine.

# Thread: Hello from Michigan
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-michigan.22649/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Yak — 2018-12-09T23:35:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-michigan.22649/post-257677>

Hello everyone, my name is Marshall. I am new to the forums and have spent the last few months pouring over the wealth of information in the blog posts, various podcasts, Vermont lectures and Patreon postings. I am 30 years old and work as a Registered Nurse in a Neurosurgical ICU in Southeastern Michigan, predominately caring for patients pre and post the sort of interventions I imagine Dr. Kruse performs in his surgical practice. The "ah-ha" moments have been countless as I read and piece together more bits of information, re-learning everything I thought I understood from a biochemistry paradigm over again but from a perspective of light, magnetism and biophysics has frankly, been a joy of learning and new incites. I have barely scratched the surface of all this information and have a full of grasp of much less but am eager to continue to learn, piece together in my head and most importantly, actually implement in life, these concepts. I am quite grateful to have access to all of this information. I have personally begun implementing CT and am lucky to have Japanese food markets in the area with a plentiful supply of oysters, roe, shrimp and any other sea vegetable or critter I could want.

Working in the dense nnEMF pit that is an ICU, working 12 hour shifts in said ICU in a Michigan winter means on the days I do work, I don't see the sun at all, as I am at work before the sun rises and home well after it sets. I have made it a point now to catch the sun rise on my off days with feet in the cold earth. The environment is far from optimal, in fact it almost seems deliberately opposite of optimal now that I begin to know better but I am more than open to altering all of those things as I am able. One last n=1 detail about myself that I would be interested to hear from others who have a better grasp of this information than I as to it effects in all of this. At the unusually young age of 9 (while growing up in Northern Florida) I was diagnosed with severe ulcerative colitis and was hospitalized frequently for it, was put on prednisone and sulfasalazine for unsuccessful management, and ultimately at age 12 the doctors decided for a total colectomy with J-pouch creation and re-anastomosis . Being so young I obviously didn't have much of a say in the matter. As it stands now I have no large intestine whatsoever and haven't since the age of 12. I have not grasped enough of the subject matter here to accurately postulate all the ways that may disadvantage or make different my attempts at optimal. Any thoughts on that would be welcomed. A wee bit long-winded of an introduction but, happy to be here to learn/implement all the same.

### caroline — 2018-12-09T23:51:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-michigan.22649/post-257678>

I actually spoke to a Delta union about this and warned them it was coming in the next few years.......and now American is feeling the effect of non-linear aspects of light by seeing medical costs rise. The power density of the network is the key missing link for the airline industry. 5G is going to kill people quickly in a plane. See CARIE FISHER as our canary in the coal mine from 2017 for what is coming soon 24/7 for us all.

Her lifestyle and job as a shift worker under blue light and nnEMF for decades gave her a low heteroplasmy rate. Heteroplasmy rates rise as our ability to generate heat in the mitochondria DECLINE.

The hotter an engine can run the more efficient it is. This is why a jet engine is more efficient than the one in your car.........but nothing man has ever built matches the efficiency of the mitochondrial matrix heat engine.

What atom is in the matrix? H+ = hydrogen in the protium isotopic form. It heats up faster than its other isotopes and it makes perfect thermodynamic sense why nature chose it over deuterium to run our hydrogen heat engine.

**_Carnot_** showed us a few hundred years ago how a hydrogen heat engine like mitochondria really operates. **He showed that the best way to increase the efficiency of a heat engine was to increase the difference in the temperature from the heat source (matrix) inside the engine and the exterior surroundings.**
Fisher's life as a shift working actor decreased the colony of mitochondria in her to make heat. As it got worse in her retinohypothalamic tract she could not make the 6 chemicals that come from POMC. One of them is _**beta-endorphin**_. When you do not make enough of this because your hydrogen heat engine runs cooler.........you begin to have to use exogenous drugs to offset the inability to make this chemical from the lack of heat your colony can create. As she lived, her heteroplasmy rate lowered further with her drug use. Another chemical made from POMC is **_alpha MSH_**. This controls appetite and your skin color. It controls how much melanin is in your skin to absorb sunlight well or poorly. Carrie was white as ghost almost her entire life. As her hydrogen heat engines failed alpha MSH crashed and she became leptin resistant and got obese. There is a deep lesson here for those not ASLEEP because of what they learned in their flawed pasts.

As heat generation capability drops, less energy is made to do the physiologic work of making chemicals our cells rely on to operate properly.

Frequent flyers will soon be wise to buy term insurance once the transition to 5G is met. In December 2018 we are at this point. Next weekend I will be bringing this story to Los Angeles to many of Ms. Fisher friends and co-workers. I expect sudden deaths, heart attacks, psychotic breaks, and blood clots to become commonplace on frequent flyers, stewardess, and pilots. Any shift working business traveler better know the real reason why Ms. Fisher had the life she did. It was all caused by how the AC power grid, blue light, and nnEMF conspired to decrease the amount of heat her colony of mitochondria made and it leads to chronic leptin resistance.

The AC power grid, with blue light nnEMF exposure (2G-5G) day or night, degrades your heat engines = HETEROPLASMY RATE. How does the cascade appear to the clinician? Melanopsin dysfunction = causes melatonin to vanish = leptin resistance = mitochondriac and melanopsin wisdom and proves ‘your’ food gurus continued downward spiral of reliability persists. They have no idea how your hydrogen heat engines operate.

Black Swan clinicians do.

More sun = more melatonin = less chance of leptin resistance. More time spent within the AC power grid, = more tech use = more screen time = more blue light = 2g-5G EXPOSURE = your colony of hydrogen heat engines cannot generate heat = sickness comes faster and death comes sooner.

### lev0phedMD — 2018-12-11T00:56:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/living-at-high-altitude-vs-lower-latitude.22660/post-257756>

My family is looking for a place to move for when I finish residency. I am considering two regions of the country.

1\. The Gulf Coast area, probably in Florida, Texas, or Alabama (below 33 N)
2\. The mountain west, specifically Wyoming, Montana, Idaho, Utah, or Colorado (and only in cities that are at 5,000 feet or higher)

The benefits of 1 = lower latitude
The drawbacks of 1 = hurricanes, not the greatest states to practice medicine, more densely populated (more nnEMF)

The benefits of 2 = high elevation (so more UV light), better areas politically, much less densely populated (less nnEMF)
The drawbacks of 1 = extremely cold for much of the year, so limited ability to do the kind of full-body naked sun exposure as 1.

I guess I'm trying to figure out exactly how much high elevation can make-up for being at a higher latitude.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-12-12T03:03:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/system-failure-in-centralized-medicine-the-evidence.22669/post-257920>

What is the evidence medicine in crumbling in a downward spiral?

The state of medicine is truly amazingly woeful. Black swans know you cannot change people but you can change the system so that people are not pushed into doing evil things. We need to break systems that no longer work for us. Boasting about the badness of any paradigm without actively involving oneself in the correction is mere madness. Become a Black swan and help me change the system.

In times of widespread chaos and confusion, it has been the duty of more advanced human beings--artists, scientists, clowns, and philosophers--to create a social order. Today’s logic machines built into software algorithms are moving faster than your brain can think. Have you ever contemplated what speed does to logic systems? Have you ever wondered if there a biologic tool to be paid by using logic machines that work faster than the logic machine in your head?
Can you keep up? Is that part of their design? Do they control narratives and beliefs just by the speed of decisions made in a tweet or post?
Social media algorithms use is about as safe for humans as drinking a vile of Mercury is when you understand information theory well.

When will we wake up to the dark side of algorithms and what they do to groups of people forced to use them?
Most nontrivial algorithms handle some kind of variable input—sorting n strings, inverting an m x n matrix, or decrypting a message with an n-bit key. Normally, the size of this input will affect the algorithm: the larger the input, the longer the running time or the more memory used.
If the relationship were always linear (so that the time increased in direct proportion to the value of n), this section wouldn't be important. However, most significant algorithms used on social media are not linear. The good news for social media is that many are sublinear and they can be engineered for control of decision making of the user.

A binary search, for example, doesn't need to look at every candidate when finding a match. The bad news is that other algorithms are considerably worse than linear; runtimes or memory requirements increase far faster than n.
An algorithm that takes a minute to process ten items may take a lifetime to process 100. So what does social media do to make sure this NEVER HAPPENS?
THEY SPEED UP THE DECISION PROCESS TO GET THE OUTCOME THEY WANT FROM YOU!!!

The intent of me posing this question for you is simply to demonstrate why using a more efficient algorithm is more useful to a social media tycoon than simply making sure the obedient idiots on the other end just own a faster computer. See that used to be the game Big Tech played on the public and why technology cycles were so fast for Intel, AMD, and Dell. But at the heart of information processing and data mining, the main question isn't about the public hardware speed or memory.........it is how fast the software algorithm is to control the decision-making process of the obedient idiot on the other end of the computer.

To this very day, the public does not see what these guys are up too........but some of us do.

I cannot tell you how many times I was told by an administrator......."Hey you signed up for this, so just fill out the call schedule and deal with it".

Part of the reason medical training is so demanding in the United States is that hospitals control the labor market for residents by assigning spots based on a centralized matching system rather than an ordinary, competitive market. While such collusive arrangements are generally prohibited by the nation’s antitrust laws, employer-controlled labor markets are not uncommon. Just as an enterprising entrepreneur [cannot form an independent baseball team](http://www.slate.com/articles/news_and_politics/history_lesson/2002/07/baseballs_con_game.html) and challenge the Yankees for a spot in the A.L. East, an aspiring doctor has no legal right or ability to negotiate the terms of his or her entrée into the medical profession. Instead, the sole avenue to being a fully licensed medical doctor in the United States is by submitting to what is known as “the match.”

[https://www.theatlantic.com/business/archive/2017/02/doctors-long-hours-schedules/516639/](https://www.theatlantic.com/business/archive/2017/02/doctors-long-hours-schedules/516639/)

### Jack Kruse — 2019-02-18T16:05:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/system-failure-in-centralized-medicine-the-evidence.22669/post-263437>

I disagree with the statement. WHY?

What if your solution makes it worse? Doing nothing is sometimes better than doing something, in medicine. In fact, this is one of the only times I think inaction has a benefit if it is used properly. When people are ill they often want to reach for a pill for their every ill because the paradigm has conditioned them to some exogenous action. This is why the supplement business is so brisk these days under the guidance of functional medicine. What people do not understand about nonaction is that not doing anything is actually doing something significant. When the clinician applies it, its successful use is highly dependent on the clinical experience. Inaction creates time for us to ponder our next action for the patient when the course is unclear. Its successful application requires experience in knowing how to apply it. 15 years ago I would have always done something and given an Rx or talked about supplementation. Now I refuse to add another uncontrolled variable to a complex unclear situation until I reflect on what is really going on in front of me. Just doing something, creates complexity and may cause a setback. A set back is a "clinical struggle" and it is less effective than doing nothing. **_We should use the downtime of inaction to think about what we are doing in these unclear moments._**

What is life? Here is a major tweet storm (75 tweets) brought on by my critiques of Huberman's podcasts that leave much to be desired from a mitochondriac perspective. It bothers me that we are defaulting to centralized PhDs ideas that are mostly half-truths. If you feel a scientist is a stranger in the public's own forest you must change something in your life immediately to make the ignorant aware of what is being missed by following the centralized dogma they pass out on social media.
Life, and, in particular, its amazing diversity spanning more than 21 orders of magnitude in size, is the most complex physical system in the universe. In spite of this, biological systems obey a host of remarkably simple and systematic empirical scaling laws which relate to how organismal features change with size over many orders of magnitude. These include fundamental quantities like metabolic rate (the rate at which energy must be supplied in order to sustain an organism), time scales (such as lifespan and heart rate), and sizes (such as length of the aorta or height of a tree trunk). It is a remarkable fact that all of these can be expressed as power-law relationships with exponents that are simple multiples of 1 (e.g. ~, 3, 3). They appear to be valid for almost all forms of life, whether it be mammalian, avian, reptilian, unicellular, or plant-like. Clearly, the universal character of these "laws" is telling us something important about the way life is organized and the constraints under which it has evolved.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/system-failure-in-centralized-medicine-the-evidence.22669/post-319016>

Medical textbooks of biochemistry are filled with beautiful diagrams to illustrate a falsehood belief that biomolecules are static actors who dance in ‘lock and key’ fashion. Biologists currently believe in this static principle of ‘recognition’ between enzymes and their respective substrates even today. They think this exists between signal molecules and their respective receptor proteins, as well as between different species of proteins.

Yet, everything in quantum theory tells us that molecules never stand still, they always move and vibrate. How would a vibrating lock and key ever work? Moreover, the most important biomolecules, proteins are no exception to this vibration rule. Indeed, the most mobile proteins are enzymes which generally have great specificities for their substrates, and the most mobile parts of the antibody molecules are precisely those containing the recognition sites for corresponding antigens.

These observations of reality are difficult to reconcile with the lock and key model.
What is more likely to be responsible for intermolecular complexes in the cellular organization is the universal electromagnetic attractive forces between molecules.

A tiny fraction of the 73 octaves present in light's spectrum would far outperform any specificity in the sizes & shapes of molecules. It is already well-known that molecules with the same intrinsic frequency of vibration not only resonate over long distances, and hence undergo coherent excitations, but can also attract one another over long distances. Quantum theory provides a better explanation of enzyme kinetics in cells.

Here is some evidence for the idea:
[https://phys.org/news/2022-06-biochemists-tool-mrna.html](https://phys.org/news/2022-06-biochemists-tool-mrna.html?fbclid=IwAR1Ay974_hg5490JGLfiIJCbnKqjgIg_5J2EVAQiW9gG4qTg66NUSwwuePM)

Cosic has provided fascinating evidence for his claim that resonance recognition is general to all protein-protein and protein-nucleic acid interactions even when substrates are absent!

[https://epjnonlinearbiomedphys.springeropen.com/articles/10.1140/epjnbp/s40366-016-0036-6](https://epjnonlinearbiomedphys.springeropen.com/articles/10.1140/epjnbp/s40366-016-0036-6)

### Jack Kruse — 2024-04-30T01:34:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/system-failure-in-centralized-medicine-the-evidence.22669/post-334109>

Decentralized science is sometimes hard, and messy, and complicated, and frustrating….... but at its core, Nature is absolutely beautiful. The same cannot be said for centralized science.

Centralized science is reductionist and rigid, bureaucratic, and driven by corporate or political agendas. It prioritizes profit over the well-being of both people and the environment, leading to unethical practices and skewed results.

In contrast, decentralized science allows for more diverse voices and perspectives to be heard, fostering creativity, innovation, and collaboration. It puts the power back in the hands of the people, allowing for more transparency and accountability in the scientific process. It is science that makes you sovereign.

Ultimately, while decentralized science may have its challenges, its potential for positive impact and genuine discovery far outweighs the limitations of centralized approaches.

# Thread: Seeking roomate(s) in Mexico!

### Kris Kolo — 2018-12-12T03:06:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seeking-roomate-s-in-mexico.22670/post-257922>

It IS possible to experiment with health (and do it comfortably and happily and reasonably inexpensively!) in Mexico. I know it seems faraway and crazy - but if I've done it, I'm assuming others can too! I started scoping out places to live in the Yukatan in October - and now am seeking one or more mito roomate(s) for 2019 in a wonderful town/ apartment I've located/ secured.

So. Details: I recently rented a large and sunny 2-bedroom apartment on the coast between Tulum and PDC - in a great Mexican resort town called Puerto Aventuras (PA). I have this apartment through November 2019.

I'm now seeking mito roommates ( i.e. individuals to take the 2nd bedroom -- one at a time of course!) to share this apt for Feb, March and/or beyond. Am willing to rent by the calendar month for 1+ months.

Price would be $900 for each calendar month (which comes to about $30/ night - not a deal you'd find elsewhere especially in Yukatan high season of Jan-May.)

Puerto Aventuras has the advantage of lower population density; it is much smaller than Cancun or PDC (5000 vs. 150,000+ in population) and is filled with interesting residents and visitors from US, Canada, Europe, Mexico and other places. It has fabulous sandy beaches with turquoise water and a lot less seaweed, high surf, and rocks than I've experienced in PDC, Tulum or other nearby beaches.

Although no place with other human beings on this coast of Mexico is perfect, my EMF "readings" with Coronet/ Trifield are a LOT lower than the readings I got in Playa del Carmen when staying there in Oct.

A plus: One can buy inexpensive, fresh fish off the fishing boats which float into PA each afternoon - and thankfully, those selling it will even clean and de-head the fish for you.

Importantly, PA has advantage of being gated and super safe - not a small issue in Mexico these days. PA is only 20 min from Playa and about 40 min from Tulum and of course is in the thick of the cenotes, many of which are right nearby.
My spacious 7-room, 2 bath, 2 bedroom apartment is on the 2nd floor of a very small 2 story condo building in a quieter part of town. It has a great common backyard/ pool area on a quiet marina where boats float by once in a while. The apartment has a private roof deck (great for sunbathing mito style!) and the backyard faces south for good sunrises. It is about 1/4 mile from the beaches and harbor shops/ restaurants (and yes - the most excellent gelateria) of Puerto Aventuras "town".

The apartment offers wired (cabled) internet and a landline phone, which i've set up for use by me/ my roomate(s). There is wireless if needed for certain situations - but I try to avoid it most of the time for health reasons. I use candles and limited red incandescent bulbs after dark.

Note: I do have a very sweet miniature golden doodle (hypoallergenic, friendly, and only 13 pounds) - so anyone who doesn't like dogs or who is allergic to dogs.... wouldn't be a good fit!

If you have questions or if you're potentially interested in sharing this apartment for one or more calendar months starting in Feb, please PM me or email me at [[email redacted]](mailto:[email redacted]).

To be fair, I'm doing first come first serve (assuming we've had a couple phone conversations to ensure mutual compatability!). I prefer female roommates but am not 100% on that. All other things being equal I'd also prefer roomates staying longer than 1 month - less turnover is easier!

### Jack Kruse — 2018-12-12T13:48:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seeking-roomate-s-in-mexico.22670/post-257964>

This might be a good opportunity for people to come to MX an spend time there for weeks at a time cheaply. Might make a good book. You should journal the experience.

### shah78 — 2018-12-13T01:36:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seeking-roomate-s-in-mexico.22670/post-258025>

welcome to the Yucatan.if _it doesn't completely transform your life ,you not doing it right!_

### Kris Kolo — 2018-12-13T04:18:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seeking-roomate-s-in-mexico.22670/post-258032>

Dr K - as much as you (and I!) hated the idea of me returning to Boston area for 5 snowy cold weeks over the holidays over the shortest days of the year, I'm SOOO excited to report I've been able to put the dark times here to good use. I convinced my husband to put our Boston suburb home on the market - and we found a hilltop house with fabulous southern/ eastern sun exposures in a small Vermont town with population under 1000. From VT, I'll be able to keep in touch with my New England work/ family / roots during the summer months when home from MX. Thx for all you encouraged me to do/ try at our consult in Cancun. I've laid groundwork with plotting/ carrying out 2 big geographic shifts, now I just have to reap benefits by immersing in sun & improved electron flows in Mexico/ VT. Kirsten

# Thread: Business ideas to believe in?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/business-ideas-to-believe-in.22671/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### JackieNewZealand — 2018-12-12T03:23:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/business-ideas-to-believe-in.22671/post-257926>

Started as an ICU nurse, preferred to try and prevent suffering, so became a Diabetes Clinical Nurse Specialist ... disillusioned with the American Diabetes Association recommendations and the dishonest monetary links to big business (kelloggs pop tarts were "ADA approved" because they were low in fat !)

So wrote a book on Diabetes and Nutrition, became a traveling speaker with Fred Pryor Seminars (internet killed that), then successfully sold supplements via affiliate marketing ... now don't believe in those anymore. What's next ? Would really like to earn an income from doing something that matters and where I can tell the truth (or at least my truth

Dr. Kruse says, as black swans, it's time to "fling our favorite monkey wrenches" into the broken system and I totally agree but unsure what to fling and where.

Have been struggling for past few months to consider next steps, Pod casting keeps popping up but maybe there are too many already. Even had thriving youtube channel with loads of subscribers but got shut down when I started talking about the dangers of vaccines. So all my work was lost in a single no judge no jury stroke of hand. But there WILL be a next chapter ... just hasn't revealed itself yet ...any suggestions greatly appreciated!

### recoen — 2018-12-12T04:48:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/business-ideas-to-believe-in.22671/post-257938>

[@JackieNewZealand](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/22660/) I am asking myself the same question currently. For now I am learning about different things I am interested in and trying to be a good mom. I hope you find your answer soon! Look for the open door

I also think there are too many podcasters, bloggers, health influencers, etc out there so I do not even consider that. I thought about an education type thing from foundational research papers. But I also do not want to rely on technology/ the internet. I am sorry to hear about your YouTube channel but am not surprised :/.

### JackieNewZealand — 2018-12-12T05:06:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/business-ideas-to-believe-in.22671/post-257940>

Yes, being a good mom is the most important job ever! Part of me just wants to go work outside somewhere and get away from this dang impersonal computer ... but the screen seems to rule our world. Have a great day.

### ElectricUniverse — 2018-12-12T20:31:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/business-ideas-to-believe-in.22671/post-257998>

If you fill a real need (there are plenty of wants but most of those are ephemeral) in society you could find happiness and an extra income.

If you remember back to your high school or college psychology classes you will recall Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs model. That's a good place to brainstorm on ideas to help others with their needs.

Today there is so much angst about financial and physical security concerns. That is where I would focus my energies, since people aren't likely to reach nirvana of 'self actualization' if they are sick, their belly is empty, or they don't have warm shelter.

Don't forget you can also go into business for yourself: learn to garden and grow your own food, devise a method of frugal living with or without a budget, learn how to repair and build household appliances, furniture, and so forth, teach yourself how to heal with medicinal herbs and all the relatively inexpensive ideas Dr Kruse has put forth here.

Good luck with your endeavors, whatever they might be.

### JackieNewZealand — 2018-12-12T21:00:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/business-ideas-to-believe-in.22671/post-258000>

Thank you for your comments. I agree. Planted 2 fig trees yesterday and my lime tree is doing well. Am very frugal because
I value my FREEDOM and without being frugal one will have to work too much ... usually for others. And yes, having a business
is definitely the way to go because reward often comes from increasing effort, unlike working for others, especially nursing ... you can work your tail off and not have any increase in reward.

### Hunger4More — 2018-12-27T02:22:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/business-ideas-to-believe-in.22671/post-259030>

Don't try to sell information - you'll go broke doing that. Give the information away for free... the funny this is people will pay you to help with the implementation.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-12-27T14:16:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/business-ideas-to-believe-in.22671/post-259054>

^^^^^100% disagree.

I think your wisdom in how you transformed from ICU nurse to educator and not into a Black Swan is what other nurses need to hear.

Transitioning is something I am beginning to realize requies a lot of time, thinking, talking to people in healthcare because once youre in this paradigm making money getting out is not easy for many.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-12-27T14:17:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/business-ideas-to-believe-in.22671/post-259055>

I think doing consultant work with nurses who are sick and tired is one way. I also think using OSHA standards for other professions and getting then into nursing is another good thing. SO being an activist for nurses on a professional level really makes some sense.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/business-ideas-to-believe-in.22671/post-259064>

A trend I am seeing on youtube is people using their daily lives to demonstrate the principles they are trying to teach. In other words, teach by showing rather than by telling. To maximize earning potential, also have a web site with blog, and write ebooks that add value to your videos.

### Billybats — 2018-12-28T00:03:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/business-ideas-to-believe-in.22671/post-259080>

I don't know how I came across them but they prohibit technology in the lower grades and I think limit the older ones so many hours a week and teach safety of them. They also teach them to learn like Dr Kruse says. Think outside the box and figure out the half truths. Its very artistic learning.

More on how emotions and cold and healing marry to one another:

Johanna used cold free diving to restore health in her bad leg. She got the message from nature that restoration of her free radical signaling in her damaged leg was the key mode in her recovery. WHAT DO ISOLATION FLOAT TANKS AND THIS FILM HAVE IN COMMON? The first paper ever written on cold water revivals was in the 1990s. We learned that it could be effective in reperfusion injury due to a variety of insults man faces. I am here to tell you that most diseases come from or are made worse by the electromagnetic environment we choose to live in because of how changes the free radical signals a cell can make in the healing callus of injured tissue.
In this way, free diving in the cold water mimics the reasons why an isolation float tank works so well in humans who have sustained injury, stress, or trauma.
Both of these techniques lower the sensory inputs to mitochondria to decrease power consumption in the defective colony of mitochondria in the damaged areas of the body. These techniques help damaged cells to limit ROS/RNS to navigate a "better solution" to mitigate the situation these cell's mitochondria are in.

No one sees this perspective EASILY, because they do not understand energy production in mitochondria and how it uses electron, proton, and photon spin numbers to make the free radical signals from the matter in these damaged cells using light and altered oxygen tensions in the damaged region.
The incident EMF in the cells during the damage and recovery phase is the key to understanding the mysteries around reperfusion injury and stress from the PVN in the brainstem. It also explains why all cases of trauma can be helped by using cold with solar frequencies. Red light is especially helpful in this arena. in the film below you'll see a cloudy sky. A cloudy sky is a red light day to a Black Swan. If you restore the cells with the proper solar EMF input you become able to limit the damaging free radical signals. Once they are lessened and the normal free radical signals can be restored to lead to healing resolution. She found her Optimal prescription was cold and free diving under cloudy days.

People in biology have no idea that all free radicals have unpaired electrons and understanding the quantum spin state of those free radicals, and how controlling light and oxygen tensions in your healing phase is the MOST IMPORTANT key to the puzzle of how you can use nature to help you win your health back. [@MattD1995](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/21586/) I think it's going to take some time to adapt. I've been doing CT on/off since 2014 (mostly consistent since 2016). I find I don't need to do nearly as much in the summer as in winter.

I think the worse your redox is, the longer it may take you to adapt, but ironically, it helps increase your redox! So kind of a bizarre situation, but definitely keep at it. I take advantage of really ramping up my CT around September so that I still have some sun to help me warm up, and then I'm ready to embrace winter coldness. Do your best, and next summer, make sure you REALLY build up your redox, and you'll be amazed how well you handle the cold the following winter.

In winter, I need a ton of CT to feel optimal. I am much more sensitive to nnEMF, blue light and other things like coffee, alcohol and carbs. CT always gives me relief of any unwanted symptoms related to poor environment: i.e. depression or fatigue.
